nameMeanings.name: Halle
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Rejean, Marcel, Andre, Arianne, Armand, Camille, Fernand, Gaspard, Laurent.
German and English: variant of   Hall  .
French (also Hallé): topographic name from Old French halle ‘covered market’, from halla ‘covered room’.

variants.name: Hall
meanings: English, Scottish, Irish, German, Norwegian, and Danish: from Middle English hall (Old English heall), Middle High German halle, Old Norse hǫll all meaning ‘hall’ (a spacious residence), hence a topographic name for someone who lived in or near a hall or an occupational name for a servant employed at a hall. In some cases it may be a habitational name from any of the places called with this word, which in some parts of Germany and Austria in the Middle Ages also denoted a salt mine. Hall is one of the commonest and most widely distributed of English surnames, bearing witness to the importance of the hall as a feature of the medieval village. The English surname has been established in Ireland since the 14th century, and, according to MacLysaght, has become numerous in Ulster since the 17th century.
Swedish: ornamental or topographic name from hall ‘hall’ (a spacious residence), or a habitational name from a placename containing the element hall ‘rock’ (from Old Norse hallr).
Chinese: variant Romanization of the surnames 何 and 賀, see   He   1 and 2.

variants.name: Hallee
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Alain, Armand, Fernand, Henri, Napoleon, Renald, Urbain.
French Canadian (also Hallée): variant of Hallé (see Halle 3).

nameMeanings.name: aula
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Salvatore, Angelo, Antonio, Giuseppe, Luigi, Pasquale.  Spanish Americo, Rosario, Felisa, Fransisco, Silvana.
Spanish: habitational name from Mula, a city in the Murcia province.
Possibly also Catalan (Mulà): topographic name from a variant of molar ‘millstone’, or a habitational name from any of several places called with this word.

variants.name: Mulic
meanings: Bosniak, Croatian, and Serbian (Mulić): patronymic from mula ‘chief judge, learned man’, a status name of Turkish origin (see Mula ).

Slovenian (Mulič) and Croatian (Mulić): probably a nickname for someone who was born out of wedlock, ultimately derived from mula ‘mule’. As a Slovenian surname, it may alternatively be e.g. a nickname derived from mula ‘sulk’ (compare Mulley ).

nameMeanings.name: Warren
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Transferred use of the surname, which is of Norman origin, a coalescence of two different surnames, one derived from a Germanic personal name based on the element war(in) ‘guard’ and the other from a place in Normandy called La Varenne ‘the game park’. The Norman personal name survived at least into the 17th century in Yorkshire, where it was particularly associated with the Scargill family. In America this name has sometimes been chosen in honour of General Joseph Warren , the first hero of the American Revolution, who was killed at Bunker Hill ( 1775 ). Among modern influences on the choice of the name has been the film actor Warren Beatty ( b. 1937 ).

en.lifeSummary: Warren de Redvers de Halle de aula was born in 1114, in Sandown, Isle of Wight, Hampshire, England, United Kingdom. He married wife Warren de Redvers de Halle before 1140, in Europe. They were the parents of at least 1 son. He died in 1176, in his hometown, at the age of 62.
